index.post2Title: The day I discovered the thrombosis

about.heading: About me
about.p1: I created this blog to document my experience with chronic pain and porto-mesenteric thrombosis.
about.p2: My goal is to turn a difficult experience into information, support, and connection with others facing health challenges.
about.p3: Here I will share real stories, everyday lessons, and reflections on care, limits, and hope.

lessons.heading: Lessons learned
lessons.intro: A record of what this journey has taught me — to remember, share, and help others going through something similar.

l1.title: Post-surgery symptoms should not be downplayed
l1.text: Intense abdominal pain, nightly fever, and difficulty eating by mouth were signs that something serious was happening — even after a seemingly routine surgery.
l1.source: The day I discovered the thrombosis

l2.title: A healthy lifestyle does not eliminate unexpected risks
l2.text: Exercise and balanced eating were part of my routine, and thrombosis still appeared. Health is not a guarantee — it is ongoing care and attention to the body.

l3.title: In hard news, calm helps protect those we love
l3.text: I did not understand everything at once, but I tried to stay calm to give my wife and family a sense of safety — even while fearing for my life.

l4.title: Follow medical instructions immediately
l4.text: "Don't move, don't lift anything, don't drive" — when the diagnosis is serious, every minute counts. Going to the hospital right away was decisive.

l5.title: Organize your family with clarity and care
l5.text: Telling my wife, arranging the trip to São Paulo, and asking my stepfather not to give details to my mother and children at first required both delicacy and speed.

l6.title: Faith can be a resource at the edge of your strength
l6.text: On the way to the hospital I almost fainted — and I prayed for strength. Spiritual support was part of what sustained me that day.

como-tudo-comecou.title: How it all began
como-tudo-comecou.p1: This is the blog's first post and the start of a conversation I wanted to have for a long time.
como-tudo-comecou.p2: Living with chronic pain and porto-mesenteric thrombosis changed my routine, my body, and how I see time.
como-tudo-comecou.p3: In this space, I want to tell this journey honestly: good days, hard days, fears, and small victories.
como-tudo-comecou.p4: If you are going through something similar, I hope this blog helps you feel less alone.

o-dia-em-que-descobri-a-trombose.title: The day I discovered the thrombosis
o-dia-em-que-descobri-a-trombose.p1: I was living a normal life: I exercised and ate a balanced diet. I had no pain.
o-dia-em-que-descobri-a-trombose.p2: After umbilical and bilateral inguinal hernia surgery, I began to feel intense abdominal pain, had difficulty eating by mouth, and ran a fever every night.
o-dia-em-que-descobri-a-trombose.p3: I remember I was lying down when my doctor called — it was a normal workday. I asked for the day off when he asked what I was doing. I answered: "I'm lying down, why?"
o-dia-em-que-descobri-a-trombose.quote: "Don't move, don't lift anything, don't drive, don't do anything… you have a massive thrombosis and I need you to come to the hospital now."
o-dia-em-que-descobri-a-trombose.p4: At first I didn't really understand what was happening. I stayed very calm and tried to pass that calm on to my wife and family. I was worried about my health and the risk of imminent death.
o-dia-em-que-descobri-a-trombose.p5: We had to organize the family to go to the hospital. Telling my wife was delicate — she was deeply worried and lost. I called my stepfather, who was with my mother and my children, and asked him not to give details: just say we needed to go to São Paulo. They said that, at that moment, my mother started to cry.
o-dia-em-que-descobri-a-trombose.p6: We packed everything quickly. I went to the hospital with my wife; I remember feeling weak, almost fainting. I prayed a lot, asking Mary and Jesus to bless me and help me get through that situation.

chegando-no-hospital.title: Arriving at the hospital
chegando-no-hospital.p1: Arriving at the hospital was calm; I felt numb, a calm before the storm.
chegando-no-hospital.p2: I was admitted and promptly taken to a room. In that setting, the doctors were already waiting for me and wanted to see me.
chegando-no-hospital.p3: That was when the roller coaster of emotions and tests began — yes, because many specialties came to see me: the gastric surgeon to find out if I needed surgery, the hematologist to understand if it was a blood disease, the vascular specialist in the circulatory system, the hepatologist who cares for the liver wanting to know if it was cancer, the nutrition team to guide my diet, the nurses with day-to-day care… phew…
chegando-no-hospital.p4: The amount of information I needed to take in was very large; my partner was by my side, Isabela. I learned much later that the conversations with the doctors were "there is a risk of death", yet everything was always handled with great lightness in front of me and I always kept an unshakeable faith that everything would work out.
